summary: The patient has severe congenital heart disease characterized by right-to-left shunt causing cyanosis at rest or exertion; they also experience symptoms such as dyspnea, fatigue, exercise intolerance, syncope, polycythemia, clubbing of fingers/toes, headache/vertigo/presyncope during physical activity, palpitations, chest pain, edema especially lower extremities, hepatomegaly, ascites if fluid overload occurs secondary to increased blood volume load. Pregnancy increases risks significantly for this individual given their underlying condition making them more susceptible to adverse outcomes including cardiac arrest and sudden death among others related directly linked issues like worsening CHF status etc., thus explaining why option A was selected over B/C/D/E where less serious forms were considered instead based upon provided information about each respective entity’s impact on overall prognosis when combined together into one scenario involving potential obstetric intervention scenarios too potentially dangerous without proper management strategies being put place first prior any decision regarding childbirth itself would otherwise pose unacceptable level danger both mother & baby alike hence ruling those options out
